questions.question: Will the U.S. have passed legislation that requires cybersecurity around AI models before 2030?
questions.resolution_criteria: Resolves to the outcome of the question found at https://www.metaculus.com/questions/17112.
questions.background:
Many AI experts worry about powerful AI getting into the hands of bad actors—those who might use such powerful AI to cause damage or for personal gain that inflicts costs on others—via hacking. A bad actor might be a black hat hacker, a terrorist group, or a rogue state.

One way to decrease the chance of this type of hacking succeeding is for those building powerful AI, the leading AI labs, to have strong cybersecurity measures in place. Given how high the stakes might be (see, e.g., “[AI risk](https://forum.effectivealtruism.org/topics/ai-risk)”), policymakers may decide to not bank on AI labs attaining strong enough cybersecurity by default.
questions.market_info_resolution_criteria: This question resolves as **Yes** if, by 2030, there is a federal law which requires all U.S. actors in possession of AI models fitting certain criteria to take measures to secure those models. The law must be designed to promote security of _powerful_ models, rather than, for example, models that might compromise individuals' privacy if stolen. If necessary, this will be decided by a judging panel made up of the Metaculus AI Forecasting team, or, failing that (e.g., because the AI Forecasting team no longer exists), at least 3 Metaculus admins: the judging panel would consider the stated intent of those who proposed the initial version of the bill.

questions.question: Will the cancer pill AOH1996 enter Phase III clinical trials before January 1, 2027?
questions.resolution_criteria: Resolves to the outcome of the question found at https://www.metaculus.com/questions/18230.
questions.background:
In [an announcement published August 1, 2023](https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/city-of-hope-scientists-develop-targeted-chemotherapy-able-to-kill-all-solid-tumors-in-preclinical-research-301888576.html), City of Hope, a cancer research organization, described the results of initial testing of AOH1996, a pill designed to [selectively kill cancer cells](https://www.cell.com/cell-chemical-biology/fulltext/S2451-9456(23)00221-0?rss=yes).

The pill has entered Phase I clinical trials, with a [study start date of August 12, 2022](https://classic.clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT05227326), and it was announced on October 19, 2022, [that the first human patient had received a dose](https://www.cityofhope.org/cancer-center-announces-first-patient-has-received-city-hopes-novel-potentially-cancer-stopping).

In a [blog post in Science](https://www.science.org/content/blog-post/new-mode-cancer-treatment) Derek Lowe says the following:

>The compound has entered a Phase I trial in humans on the basis of the above data, and I very much look forward to seeing it advance to Phase II, where it will doubtless be used in combination with several existing therapies.

[Antidote describes clinical trial phases as follows](https://www.antidote.me/blog/how-long-do-clinical-trial-phases-take):

>**Phase 1:** Phase 1 of a clinical trial will typically be open to 20 and 100 participants, and will last for several months. This phase is not intended to evaluate whether the treatment in question is effective — instead, the purpose of this phase is to measure safety by checking for adverse side effects. Phase 1 trials typically look for healthy volunteers, but may seek people with the condition being studied depending on the type of research in question.
>
>**Phase 2:** Once a drug has been deemed safe from the Phase 1 results, it is able to progress to Phase 2. Around 70% of drugs progress to the second phase, which is designed to measure safety in a larger group of volunteers (typically several hundred), while also measuring the effectiveness of the treatment on the participants.
>
>**Phase 3:** About 33% of drugs will make it past the second phase and go on to Phase 3. The third phase of a clinical trial recruits the largest number of participants, with enrollment goals typically in the thousands. Phase 3 is intended to measure both safety and efficacy for a longer duration, usually lasting between one and four years.
questions.market_info_resolution_criteria: This question will resolve as **Yes** if, before January 1, 2027, [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/help/faq/#definitions) report that AOH1996 has begun Phase III clinical trials.

questions.question: Before 2027, will a federal court of appeals overturn a pre-Loper Bright decision that relied on Chevron deference, citing the Supreme Court’s ruling in Loper Bright as the primary reason?
questions.resolution_criteria: Resolves to the outcome of the question found at https://www.metaculus.com/questions/26623.
questions.background:
In June 2024, the U.S. Supreme Court [overturned](https://insideclimatenews.org/news/28062024/supreme-court-overturns-chevron-doctrine/) the Chevron doctrine in a 6-3 decision in [Loper Bright Enterprises v. Raimondo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Loper_Bright_Enterprises_v._Raimondo). The Chevron doctrine had held that courts should defer to federal agencies' reasonable interpretations of ambiguous statutes. 

According to the [New York Times](https://www.nytimes.com/2024/06/28/us/politics/chevron-deference-decision-meaning.html):

>...thousands of legal decisions have relied on the Chevron doctrine when challenges have been made to regulations stemming from laws like the 1938 Fair Labor Standards Act, the 1970 Clean Air Act, the 2010 Affordable Care Act and others.

However, some legal experts have said that it may not be so simple to overturn previous rulings. According to lawyers writing for the firm of [Cleary Gottlieb](https://www.clearygottlieb.com/news-and-insights/publication-listing/after-chevron-what-the-supreme-courts-loper-bright-decision-changed-and-what-it-didnt): 

>...the Court stated that the mere fact that a prior case relied on Chevron is not a sufficient basis for overturning it now.  Stare decisis is an especially powerful consideration in the context of statutory consideration, and Loper Bright sought to impose limits on a potential flood of lawsuits challenging decades worth of precedents based on Chevron.  The Court cautioned that “[m]ere reliance on Chevron cannot constitute a special justification” that would justify “overruling such a holding.” Accordingly, plaintiffs will need to do more than simply point to Loper Bright when seeking to challenge an regulation that once relied on Chevron.
questions.market_info_resolution_criteria:
The question resolves as **Yes** if, before January 1, 2027, [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/help/faq/#definitions) report that a federal court of appeals has published an opinion that meets these two specific criteria:

1. The opinion explicitly overturns any other pre-[Loper Bright](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Loper_Bright_Enterprises_v._Raimondo) decision that relied on the doctrine of [Chevron deference](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chevron_U.S.A.,_Inc._v._Natural_Resources_Defense_Council,_Inc.). An opinion that merely overturns a pre–Loper Bright decision on direct appellate review will not trigger positive resolution of this question.

2. The opinion cites the Supreme Court’s Loper Bright ruling as a primary reason or doing so.

If this does not happen before January 1, 2027, this question resolves as **No**.

questions.question: Will President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva be reelected as Brazil's president in October of 2026?
questions.resolution_criteria: Resolves to the outcome of the question found at https://www.metaculus.com/questions/22310.
questions.background:
Lula has been elected thrice for the presidency in Brazil, in 2002, 2006, and 2022. All three times he led the first round with over 40% of votes, winning then in the second round with 60% of votes the first two times, and with 51% in 2022. Current election law requires a candidate to get more than 50% of valid votes in either the first round or the runoff to be elected.

Lula's party (PT - Workers' Party) has won every presidential election held this century with the exception of 2018, when Lula was imprisoned for corruption charges and was unable to run. His protege and current Finance Minister, Fernando Haddad, lost to Jair Bolsonaro, who then went on to lose to Lula in 2022, becoming the first incumbent president to lose reelection in Brazil (out of four presidents who attempted and five who were eligible). Bolsonaro himself is currently unable to run until 2030 due to election interference charges.
questions.market_info_resolution_criteria: This resolves YES if Lula is declared the winner of Brazil's Presidential Election of 2026 by the Superior Electoral Court.

questions.question: #3 - Will the Tiger Point Wastewater Treatment Facility expansion be finished before February 2027?
questions.resolution_criteria: Resolves to the outcome of the question found at https://www.metaculus.com/questions/38950.
questions.background:
Gulf Breeze is expanding the Tiger Point Wastewater Treatment Facility to help serve more homes and support future growth. The project will increase the facility's capacity from 2.0 to 3.5 million gallons per day—allowing more households to move off septic systems and connect to city sewer.

Construction began in August 2024, and as of mid-2025, the work was about 25% complete. The expansion is being funded by a mix of state and federal dollars, plus a new \$14 monthly fee for utility customers.

City plans show the project is scheduled to be finished by January 2027. This forecast asks: will the construction be complete before February 1, 2027?

**Resources**

Gulf Breeze operates an active stormwater management program designed to reduce pollution and flooding, managed under the EPA’s Municipal Separate Storm Sewer System (MS4) permit requirements ([City of Gulf Breeze Stormwater Management](https://cityofgulfbreeze.us/departments/public-works/stormwater-management/)).

The Florida Department of Environmental Protection’s 2025-2026 Resilient Florida Annual Plan outlines substantial investments in flood resilience and shoreline protection statewide, with programs that fund projects to harden coastal infrastructure against flooding and hurricanes ([FDEP Resilient Florida 2025-2026 Plan](https://floridadep.gov/sites/default/files/25-26%20Resilient%20Florida%20Annual%20Plan_1.pdf)).

Santa Rosa County, including Gulf Breeze, was awarded over \$8.5 million in state funding through the Local Government Water Supply Pilot Grant Program to improve water infrastructure and resilience in 2024-2025, highlighting the region’s capacity to secure sizable grants for environmental infrastructure ([Santa Rosa County Grant Announcement](https://www.santarosa.fl.gov/CivicAlerts.asp?AID=1701\&ARC=2167)).

The state’s broader water quality and infrastructure programs also provide grant opportunities supporting stormwater and shoreline resiliency projects, which Gulf Breeze is well positioned to pursue ([Protecting Florida Together Grants](https://protectingfloridatogether.gov/state-action/grants-submissions)).
questions.market_info_resolution_criteria:
![](https://cdn.metaculus.com/user_uploaded/Screenshot_2025-08-16_at_1.17.50AM.png)

Scroll down to the Background section for more on this question and why it's important for our community.

This question will resolve **Yes** if the City of Gulf Breeze officially reports that the Tiger Point Wastewater Treatment Facility expansion is substantially complete before February 1, 2027. Otherwise, it will resolve **No**. Official city updates or public project documents will be used for confirmation.

questions.question: Before June 2027, will a top-level French official publicly state that France will adopt a Sixth Republic?
questions.resolution_criteria: Resolves to the outcome of the question found at https://www.metaculus.com/questions/39820.
questions.background:
France has been in one of its worst political crises under the 5th Republic since the [dissolution of the Assembly](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Parliamentary_dissolution_\(France\)#Emmanuel_Macron's_dissolution "dissolution of the Assembly") by Emmanuel Macron in June 2024, bringing the end of the Attal government and a new Assembly even more split than before. Before that government, [the average duration](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Premier_ministre_fran%C3%A7ais#Dur%C3%A9es_%C3%A0_Matignon) of a Prime Minister since 1958 was above 30 months; the average over the past decade is now down to 17 months.

Since June 2024, the Barnier government has been censored by the Assembly and been replaced by the Bayrou government, which fell too after [losing a vote of confidence](https://www.lemonde.fr/en/politics/article/2025/09/08/francois-bayrou-ousted-as-french-pm-after-losing-confidence-vote_6745163_5.html "losing the vote of confidence") on September 8, 2025. Sebastien Lecornu has now been nominated as Prime Minister on September 9, 2025, but the length of his tenure is very uncertain with [many obstacles already in his way](https://apnews.com/article/france-new-prime-minister-lecornu-protests-budget-5cbea14cd492d2893afbd3339c52b3d2). Several parties (RN, LFI, Green) are asking for a new legislative election or the President's resignation, while others ready to negotiate (LR, PS) seem unlikely to be enough to get a majority.

While the 1958 Constitution was designed to stop incessant changes to the Prime Minister from occurring again, a similar instability was the reason for the end of the 4th Republic. That context is now bringing more and more people to consider the possibility of switching to a 6th Republic.
questions.market_info_resolution_criteria:
This question will resolve as **Yes** if the French President, Prime Minister, or a cabinet minister make an on-the-record public statement before June 1, 2027, that France will adopt a ‘Sixth Republic.’

A public statement will *not* count, however, if a retraction or refutation is made within 24 hours by the official who made the comment, the Prime Minister or the President.

questions.question: Will the Insurrection Act or a state of martial law be declared in relation to the 2026 U.S. midterms?
questions.resolution_criteria: Resolves to the outcome of the question found at https://www.metaculus.com/questions/41563.
questions.background:
*This forecasting question is associated with the Verity controversy:  Will the Insurrection Act or a state of martial law be declared in relation to the 2026 U.S. midterms?*

The [<u>Insurrection Act of 1807</u>](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Insurrection_Act_of_1807#:~:text=The%20Insurrection%20Act%20of%201807,insurrection%2C%20and%20of%20armed%20rebellion) is a federal law that allows the U.S. president to deploy active-duty military forces or federalize the National Guard within the United States under specific circumstances, such as suppressing insurrection, enforcing federal law when states are unwilling or unable to do so, or protecting constitutional rights. Unlike most emergency powers, the Insurrection Act places [<u>few procedural constraints on presidential discretion</u>](https://www.brennancenter.org/our-work/research-reports/insurrection-act-explained), which has led to longstanding debate over its scope and potential for abuse. U.S. courts have repeatedly held that martial law is constitutionally suspect and permissible only in the most extraordinary circumstances, particularly [<u>where civilian courts remain functional</u>](https://supreme.justia.com/cases/federal/us/71/2/).

The Insurrection Act has been invoked sparingly in modern history, most notably during [<u>desegregation efforts in the 1950s–60s and during the 1992 Los Angeles riots</u>](https://www.brennancenter.org/our-work/research-reports/guide-invocations-insurrection-act). More recently, its potential use has been [<u>publicly discussed</u>](https://www.pbs.org/newshour/politics/what-is-the-insurrection-act-heres-what-trump-has-said-about-using-it) — though not invoked — in response to civil unrest and election-related disputes, prompting renewed scrutiny from lawmakers and civil-military scholars.

With reference to the [<u>2026 U.S. midterm elections</u>](https://gsi.s-rminform.com/articles/trouble-ahead-rising-political-tensions-foreshadow-2026-us-midterm-elections), questions about the Insurrection Act or martial law typically arise in relation to concerns about [<u>election administration, civil unrest, contested results, or disruptions to voting or certification processes</u>](https://www.brennancenter.org/our-work/analysis-opinion/insurrection-act-presidential-power-threatens-democracy#:~:text=And%20let's%20not%20forget%20Trump's,in%20Spanish%20by%20La%20Opini%C3%B3n.). U.S. elections are administered by states, and federal military involvement in election-related matters would represent a significant departure from normal practice. As a result, any invocation of the Insurrection Act or declaration resembling martial law tied to the 2026 midterms would likely trigger [<u>immediate legal challenges</u>](https://www.cnn.com/2025/10/21/politics/fact-check-insurrection-act-trump), congressional scrutiny, and [<u>domestic and international attention.</u>](https://www.cbc.ca/news/world/us-trump-insurrection-act-threat-9.6931273)
questions.market_info_resolution_criteria:
This question will resolve as Yes if, before January 20, 2027, one or more of the following occur:

1. The President of the United States invokes the Insurrection Act officially citing election-related reasons.
2. Federal or state authorities declare martial law or establish military control over civilian governance for election-related reasons, with the reasons acknowledged by official government statements.
3. A U.S. court, Congress, or a federal agency formally recognizes that military authority has replaced or superseded civilian election administration in one or more jurisdictions as a result of executive action that is recognised to be tied to election-related reasons.

questions.question: Will Mike Johnson remain Speaker of the House until January 3, 2027?
questions.resolution_criteria: Resolves to the outcome of the question found at https://www.metaculus.com/questions/40976.
questions.background:
The 119th United States Congress convened on January 3, 2025, and [re-elected the 56th Speaker of the House Republican Mike Johnson](https://www.speaker.gov/2025/01/04/house-adopts-rules-for-the-119th-congress/). Republicans began with a slim majority 219 to 215 (with 1 vacant).

Despite being the Speaker of the House since October 25, 2023, Johnson has recently come under pressure from his own party. As [reported by Politico](https://www.politico.com/news/2025/12/04/mike-johnson-ndaa-pentagon-bill-00677847) on December 4, 2025, House Republicans are "questioning the speaker’s hold on his tenuous House majority. Next week, he has to prove he’s capable of governing."

> The annual Pentagon policy bill is due on the floor just in time to test Johnson’s ability to command and cajole his conference with must-pass legislation at stake. Already GOP leaders have had to [delay release of bill text](https://www.politico.com/news/2025/12/03/ndaa-delays-defense-bill-00676098) as they deal with a host of 11th-hour intraparty flare-ups that show just how hard it will be for the speaker to lead ahead of next year’s midterm elections. 

> But the pending fights over the Pentagon bill — or the National Defense Authorization Act, as it is formally known — serve as a mini-preview for the turmoil Johnson is likely to face for the remainder of the 119th Congress as he tries to tackle health care, government funding and other flashpoints.

As [Axios also reports](https://www.axios.com/2025/12/04/mike-johnson-stefanik-mace-internal-revolt), "the pattern \[of internal revolt] mirrors the internal dynamics that destabilized former Speaker Kevin McCarthy. Johnson isn't currently facing a motion to vacate, but the escalating public criticism is a troubling sign for the two-term speaker."
questions.market_info_resolution_criteria: This question will resolve as **Yes** if Mike Johnson remains Speaker of the US House of Representatives from December 13, 2025, continuously until the end of the 119th Congress, on January 3, 2027. If he is not Speaker of the House at any point before the end of the 119th Congress, this question will resolve as **No**.

questions.question: Will OpenAI, DeepMind, or Anthropic have revenue of at least $100B in 2027?
questions.resolution_criteria: Resolves to the outcome of the question found at https://www.metaculus.com/questions/38405.
questions.background:
In [AI 2027's](https://ai-2027.com) [*C*](https://ai-2027.com/research/compute-forecast#section-5-industry-metrics)[*ompute Forecast*](https://ai-2027.com/research/compute-forecast#section-5-industry-metrics)[,](https://ai-2027.com/research/compute-forecast#section-5-industry-metrics) annual revenue of OpenBrain - a fictionalised company whom AI 2027 expects to be the leading world's leading AI company in terms of computing power and model performance - is forecasted to be \$140 billion. The leading AI companies today are considered to be [OpenAI](https://openai.com), Google's [DeepMind](https://deepmind.google) and [Anthropic](https://www.anthropic.com).

Section 5 of the *Compute Forecast* provides AI 2027's methodology for this \$140 billion figure which ultimately leans heavily on OpenAI's financials and subscriber model growth:

> We use OpenAI’s [2023 revenue of \$1B](https://www.businessinsider.com/openai-cfo-revenue-forecast-chatgpt-2025-2) and [2024 revenue around \$4B](https://www.nytimes.com/2024/09/27/technology/openai-chatgpt-investors-funding.html) to to piece together a short term trend that we expect to slow down gradually, but see sustained exponential growth through 2027 due to agentic models attracting high paying subscribers for products such as ‘drop in remote workers.’

|                                            |   2023 | 2024 |  2025 |  2026 |   2027 |
| ------------------------------------------ | -----: | ---: | ----: | ----: | -----: |
| Annual Revenue, billion \$USD              |   \$1B | \$4B | \$14B | \$45B | \$140B |
| Revenue Year-on Year Growth, %             |      - | 300% |  250% |  221% |   211% |
| Annual Compute Cost, billion \$USD         | \$1.8B | \$6B | \$16B | \$40B | \$100B |
| Annual Compute Cost Year-on-Year Growth, % |      - | 233% |  166% |  150% |    50% |

***

AI 2027 notes some key factors:

* *Compute Cost.* How much a capital a company assigns to computational spending (i.e., model training costs).
* *Computation Price Performance.* How much computational power you receive with respect to cost - measured in Floating-point Operations Per Second (FLOP) per dollar cost (FLOP/\$)
* *AI Agents.* Subscribers of models are the principal source of revenue for AI companies. Both AI 2027 and FutureSearch forecast that AI Agent "replacement workers" may be a substantial source of revenue for leading AI companies.

***

See also:

FutureSearch. *OpenAI’s path to sufficient revenue for an AI takeoff in 2027.* April 3, 2025. Accessed at: [https://futuresearch.ai/openbrain-revenue](https://futuresearch.ai/openbrain-revenue)
questions.market_info_resolution_criteria: This question will resolve as **Yes** if at least one of OpenAI, DeepMind or Anthropic have an annualised revenue of at least \$100 billion in 2027.

questions.question: Will federal courts block the USPS mail-in ballot restrictions before Election Day?
questions.resolution_criteria: Resolves to the outcome of the question found at https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43527.
questions.background:
Mail-in voting, also known as postal voting, has a long history [in the United States](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Postal_voting_in_the_United_States), though it has increased in popularity and salience since the coronavirus pandemic prompted [many more Americans](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Postal_voting_in_the_2020_United_States_elections) to vote by mail in 2020. At the same time, U.S. President Donald Trump has repeatedly and for years [expressed concern](https://www.npr.org/2020/08/19/903886567/trump-while-attacking-mail-voting-casts-mail-ballot-again) about mail-in voting, suggesting that it creates too great an opportunity for election fraud.

Every American state offers at least some form of mail-in voting: In many states, voters may choose to vote by mail for any reason at all. In some other states, voters may vote by mail only if they have a [statutorily approved reason](https://www.ncsl.org/elections-and-campaigns/table-2-excuses-to-vote-absentee) (for example, because they will be traveling on Election Day, because they are unwell or disabled, or even simply because they are aged). Finally, in still other states, elections are held entirely via mail, and all eligible voters receive a mail-in ballot without needing to request one.

Nevertheless, on March 31, 2026, President Trump issued [an executive order](https://www.whitehouse.gov/presidential-actions/2026/03/ensuring-citizenship-verification-and-integrity-in-federal-elections/) calling on the Postmaster General to issue regulations requiring individuals to enroll with the United States Postal Service in order to be eligible to transmit a ballot through the mail. The executive order also calls on the Secretary of Homeland Security to assemble lists of U.S. citizens age 18 and order in each state and to send those lists to each state's election officials.

This executive action was immediately challenged in federal court. On April 1, 2026, various organs of the Democratic Party [filed suit](https://www.democracydocket.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/1-2026-04-01-Complaint-2.pdf) in federal district court in Washington, D.C. On April 2, the League of Women Voters (LWV) and other organizations [filed suit](https://www.democracydocket.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/2026-04-02-Complaint.pdf) in federal district court in Massachusetts, and the League of United Latin American Citizens (LULAC) and other organizations [filed suit](https://www.democracydocket.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/2026-04-02-Complaint-1.pdf) in D.C. federal court. On April 3, [various blue states](https://www.democracydocket.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/2026-04-03-Complaint-1.pdf) also filed suit in Massachusetts, and [the NAACP and other organizations](https://www.democracydocket.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/gov.uscourts.dcd_.291146.1.0DD.pdf) also filed suit in D.C.

The three lawsuits filed in D.C. were consolidated, and a group of red states were allowed to intervene as additional defendants. The consolidated cases are currently being heard by [Judge Carl Nichols](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carl_J._Nichols), a Trump appointee. On May 28, 2026, Judge Nichols [denied plaintiffs' request for a preliminary injunction](https://fingfx.thomsonreuters.com/gfx/legaldocs/zdvxgzbdnpx/DCD%20-%20Mail-in%20ballot%20ruling%2020260528.pdf), stating that until the Postal Service took further action, the plaintiffs' claims were not ripe.

The two lawsuits filed in Massachusetts are both before [Judge Indira Talwani](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indira_Talwani), an appointee of President Barack Obama, who has refused to transfer the cases to the D.C. court. The two lawsuits have been partially consolidated, and Judge Talwani has allowed the group of red states to intervene in the LWV's suit but not in the suit brought by blue states (though the red states are currently appealing that latter decision). Judge Talwani is scheduled to hear arguments on the LWV plaintiffs' request for a preliminary injunction and the blue states' request for a permanent injunction on June 2, 2026.

Decisions granting or denying injunctions [may be appealed immediately](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/28/1292).
questions.market_info_resolution_criteria: This question will resolve as **Yes** if, on Election Day (November 3, 2026), a federal court order (e.g., injunction, stay, or vacatur) is in effect that prevents the federal government from restricting which voters' mail-in or absentee ballots the United States Postal Service will transmit for the 2026 general election.

questions.background:
The [FIFA World Cup](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup) is a quadrennial [football](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Association_football) competition organised by FIFA. The [2026 edition](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2026_FIFA_World_Cup), set to be jointly hosted Canada, Mexico and the United States, will take place from June 11 to July 19 2026.

questions.question: Will Instructure be subject to another major hack before September 1, 2026?
questions.resolution_criteria: Resolves to the outcome of the question found at https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43501.
questions.background:
Instructure is the company behind Canvas LMS, used by [41%](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2026_Canvas_security_incident) of North American higher education institutions for coursework, exams, grading, and student communication. In the last eight months, it has been hacked twice by [ShinyHunters](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ShinyHunters), a black-hat criminal hacking and extortion group.

In September 2025, ShinyHunters [accessed Instructure's Salesforce environment](https://www.instructure.com/resources/blog/security-incident-update), compromising business contact information. Then in late April 2026, the group [exploited a vulnerability in Canvas's Free-For-Teacher accounts](https://www.theregister.com/cyber-crime/2026/05/12/congress-investigates-canvas-breach-after-instructure-cuts-deal-with-shinyhunters/5238927) to exfiltrate an estimated 3.65 terabytes of data — including names, emails, student IDs, and billions of private messages — affecting approximately 275 million users across 8,809 institutions. After Instructure attempted to patch the vulnerability without negotiating, the ShinyHunters [re-entered on May 7 through the same vulnerability](https://www.theregister.com/cyber-crime/2026/05/12/congress-investigates-canvas-breach-after-instructure-cuts-deal-with-shinyhunters/5238927), defacing login pages with ransom demands and triggering a global Canvas outage during exams week, leading many universities to postpone finals. Instructure [ultimately paid a ransom](https://www.insidehighered.com/news/tech-innovation/administrative-tech/2026/05/11/instructure-pays-ransom-canvas-hackers) in exchange for data destruction and assurances against further extortion.

[At least three class-action lawsuits have been filed](https://www.abc4.com/news/wasatch-front/lawsuits-instructure-data-breach/) alleging Instructure failed to protect user data (including that of minors) and that the outage cut off access to materials students needed to study for finals. The [U.S. House Homeland Security Committee has summoned CEO Steve Daly](https://techcrunch.com/2026/05/13/us-lawmakers-demand-answers-from-instructure-after-canvas-data-breaches/) for a briefing by May 21, 2026, and the [FBI, CISA, and Department of Education are all engaged](https://fsapartners.ed.gov/knowledge-center/library/electronic-announcements/2026-05-12/technology-security-alert-ongoing-cybersecurity-incident-involving-canvas-learning-management-system). Instructure [states that Canvas is fully operational with enhanced security measures](https://www.instructure.com/incident_update), though its forensic investigation remains ongoing.

Cybersecurity experts have [noted that paying the ransom risks incentivizing future attacks](https://www.insidehighered.com/news/tech-innovation/administrative-tech/2026/05/11/instructure-pays-ransom-canvas-hackers). The sheer concentration of educational infrastructure on a single platform means another breach would compound reputational, legal, and operational damage at a scale difficult to absorb.
questions.market_info_resolution_criteria:
This question will resolve as **Yes** if, after the question's opening and before September 1, 2026, Instructure or any of its [products](https://www.instructure.com/products) (including but not limited to Canvas, Mastery, Parchment, and Impact) experiences at least one cybersecurity incident that meets any of the following conditions:

1. **Data exfiltration:** An unauthorized party obtains personal data (such as names, email addresses, student IDs, messages, credentials, or financial information) of at least 10,000 users.
2. **Service disruption:** One or more Instructure products are rendered substantially unavailable to at least 100 institutional customers for a continuous period of at least 4 hours as a direct result of a security breach.
3. **Ransom or extortion demand:** A threat actor issues a ransom or extortion demand related to data or systems access newly obtained from Instructure.

All conditions require confirmation from at least one of the following: Instructure official communications, credible news reporting, or government disclosures. An attacker's public claim alone will not be sufficient.

questions.question: Will the Federal Reserve raise the federal funds target range before July 30, 2026?
questions.resolution_criteria: Resolves to the outcome of the question found at https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43688.
questions.background:
CNBC May 2026: [The Fed will have to raise interest rates in July to appease ‘bond vigilantes,’ Yardeni says](https://www.cnbc.com/2026/05/18/the-fed-will-have-to-raise-interest-rates-in-july-to-appease-bond-vigilantes-yardeni-says.html)

>In statements prior to taking over the chair’s position, \[Fed Chair Kevin] [Warsh has said he believes](https://www.cnbc.com/2026/05/16/kevin-warsh-comes-into-the-fed-facing-a-big-family-fight-over-cutting-interest-rates.html) the Fed can lower its benchmark interest rate from its current targeted range of 3.5% to 3.75%. However, a [recent surge in inflation](https://www.cnbc.com/2026/05/12/cpi-inflation-april-2026-.html), triggered largely by the Iran war but also owning to other underlying factors, has led to markets repricing rate expectations. But it’s gotten more complicated with Warsh’s arrival: Not only does the market not believe the Fed will cut, but odds also are rising for a hike, with current pricing implying a 42% chance of an increase by the end of the year, according to the CME Group’s [FedWatch](https://www.cmegroup.com/markets/interest-rates/cme-fedwatch-tool.html?redirect=/trading/interest-rates/countdown-to-fomc.html) tool. \[Market veteran Ed] Yardeni, though, thinks it will come sooner than that. While he sees the Fed holding steady at the June meeting, he believes a rate increase of a quarter percentage point is “likely” in July.
questions.market_info_resolution_criteria: This question resolves as **Yes** if, before July 30, 2026, the United States Federal Reserve announces an increase in the federal funds [target range](https://www.federalreserve.gov/aboutthefed/fedexplained/accessible-version.htm) relative to 3.5%–3.75%.

questions.question: Will OpenAI's ChatGPT Atlas browser be released for Windows before September 1, 2026?
questions.resolution_criteria: Resolves to the outcome of the question found at https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43525.
questions.background:
On October 21, 2025, OpenAI [released](https://openai.com/index/introducing-chatgpt-atlas/) a web browser built around ChatGPT, called [ChatGPT Atlas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ChatGPT_Atlas):

> With Atlas, ChatGPT can come with you anywhere across the web—helping you in the window right where you are, understanding what you’re trying to do, and completing tasks for you, all without copying and pasting or leaving the page.

Early reviews suggest that Atlas [shows promise](https://www.pcmag.com/news/chatgpts-atlas-browser-shows-potential-but-its-no-chrome-killeryet) but is not yet worth switching to. And some have suggested that it is [particularly vulnerable to hacking](https://www.theregister.com/2025/10/27/atlas_vulnerability_memory_injection/).

In releasing Atlas, OpenAI [opened another front](https://techcrunch.com/2025/10/21/openai-launches-an-ai-powered-browser-chatgpt-atlas/) in its rivalry with Google, who makes not only ChatGPT competitor [Gemini](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Google_Gemini) but also [Chrome](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Google_Chrome), by far [the world's most popular web browser](https://gs.statcounter.com/browser-market-share). Atlas was initially made available only for macOS, but OpenAI has promised that "Experiences for Windows, iOS, and Android are coming soon." That will be a must if OpenAI wants to take a chunk out of Chrome's market share.

On January 21, 2026, OpenAI [released new features](https://www.pcmag.com/news/chatgpt-atlas-gets-new-tab-upgrades-still-no-windows-or-mobile-versions) for Atlas but has not provided an updated time-frame for the anticipated Windows release.
questions.market_info_resolution_criteria: This question will resolve as **Yes** if, before September 1, 2026 UTC, OpenAI publicly releases ChatGPT Atlas for Windows.

questions.question: Bude mít ElevenLabs do 30.6. 2027 valuaci alespoň 30 miliard USD?
questions.resolution_criteria: Resolves to the outcome of the question found at https://www.metaculus.com/questions/42033.
questions.background:
[ElevenLabs](https://elevenlabs.io/) je firmou z oblasti generativní AI zaměřená na syntézu hlasu (text-to-speech), *voice cloning* a tvorbu audio nástrojů pro tvůrce i firmy. Její hlavní službou je nabídka API a aplikace, díky kterým lze generovat přirozeně znějící řeč v mnoha jazycích a stylech, případně vytvářet hlasové modely (např. pro dabing, audioknihy, zákaznické linky, hlasové asistenty a „AI agenty“). Společnost byla založena v roce 2022 a rychle získala pozornost kvalitou výstupu a jednoduchou integrací pro vývojáře i mediální domy. Mezi uživatele patří i řada zaměstnanců Fortune 500 firem.

Po startu v roce 2023 následoval rychlý fundraising a skoky ve valuacích. V lednu 2024 se v médiích psalo o [Series B](https://elevenlabs.io/blog/series-b) (80 mil. USD) a valuaci kolem 1,1 mld. USD. V lednu 2025 firma oznámila [Series C](https://elevenlabs.io/blog/series-c) (180 mil. USD) při valuaci 3,3 mld. USD. V září 2025 pak proběhl zaměstnanecký sekundární prodej/tender při valuaci 6,6 mld. USD. Reuters u toho zároveň popsal výrazný růst ARR (řádově stovky milionů USD) a rychlý nábor personálu, což jsou typické signály, které trh používá k ospravedlnění vyšších valuací, zároveň jde o sektor se silnou konkurencí a regulatorními/reputačními riziky (zneužití hlasových deepfaků, autentizace, souhlasy a práva k hlasu). Podle investorů, se kterými jsme pro tuto výzvu v kontaktu, už nyní přesahuje hodnota ElevenLabs 10 mld. USD.
questions.market_info_resolution_criteria:
Otázku vyhodnotíme jako ANO, pokud nejpozději do 30. 6. 2027 dojde k veřejně potvrzené transakci, která explicitně uvádí valuaci ElevenLabs ve výši alespoň 30 mld. USD, a tato transakce je buď:

a) primární investiční kolo (funding round, např. Series D apod.), nebo
b) sekundární prodej/tender (např. zaměstnanecká likvidita, prodej podílů investorů), kde je valuace výslovně uvedena.

„Veřejně potvrzené“ znamená alespoň jeden z těchto zdrojů:

* oficiální oznámení ElevenLabs (blog, press release, investiční update),
* oznámení vedoucího investora / organizátora tenderu, nebo
* vysoce důvěryhodné finanční zpravodajství (typicky Reuters/Bloomberg/FT) s konkrétní valuací.

questions.question: Will GiveWell recommend 1 or more grants to support breastfeeding promotion or denote it a "top charity" before 2027?
questions.resolution_criteria: Resolves to the outcome of the question found at https://www.metaculus.com/questions/8408.
questions.background:
GiveWell has recommended grants to over 10 charities over the years. They are currently investigating [14 charity areas](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1TG7WRU85p1SEjir-5qvIEg4kVG9a4Lnzdgwcub8aKSs/edit#gid=0) including breastfeeding promotion.

The following sections are quoted from GiveWell’s [explanation of the topic](https://www.givewell.org/international/technical/programs/breastfeeding-promotion):

“The World Health Organization (WHO) and UNICEF recommend early initiation of breastfeeding, exclusive breastfeeding to 6 months, and partial breastfeeding to age 24 months to improve infant and maternal health, but the majority of infants are not fed according to these guidelines. Mothers may not breastfeed as long or as intensively as they would like due to a lack of skills and support, so various maternal counselling and support interventions promote breastfeeding behaviour change.”

Effectiveness for the following reasons: “We believe there is reasonably strong evidence that breastfeeding support programs can lead to increases in rates of exclusive breastfeeding up to 6 months (compared to some or predominant breastfeeding) and breastfeeding duration, as recommended by WHO/UNICEF. Their impacts on exclusive breastfeeding may be larger in low-income countries. There is additional evidence that increasing breastfeeding reduces diarrhoea morbidity, which likely leads to reductions in childhood mortality from diarrhoea. It may also cause additional benefits that we have not yet vetted.”
questions.market_info_resolution_criteria:
This question resolves positively if GiveWell publishes a recommendation that grants be given to support breastfeeding promotion or on or before December 31, 2026. Or if a breastfeeding promotion charity is designated a top charity before December 31, 2026.

This may happen in the following ways:

- GiveWell writes “yes” in the column “Have we recommended one or more grants to support this program?”  in the “breastfeeding promotion” row of the GiveWell [program reviews spreadsheet](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1TG7WRU85p1SEjir-5qvIEg4kVG9a4Lnzdgwcub8aKSs/edit#gid=0) or the spreadsheet which supersedes this one (see below). 
- Givewell publishes a blog in which which they announce they are recommending a grant in the area of breastfeeding promotion
- Givewell adds an incubation grant to a breastfeeding promotion charity on this page [https://www.givewell.org/research/incubation-grants](https://www.givewell.org/research/incubation-grants)
- A GiveWell blog post announcing that a charity in the area of breastfeeding promotion is a “Top Charity”
- GiveWell publishes a section on breastfeeding promotion on its [Top Charities](https://www.givewell.org/charities/top-charities) page

questions.question: Will total MLB regular-season attendance in 2026 exceed 72 million?
questions.resolution_criteria: Resolves to the outcome of the question found at https://www.metaculus.com/questions/42741.
questions.background:
Total MLB regular-season attendance reached record highs in the mid-2010s, topping 73 million several times. It dropped sharply during the COVID-19 pandemic but has recovered steadily since, reaching 70,747,365 in 2023, 71,348,366 in 2024, and 71,409,421 in 2025. The last time total attendance was above 72 million was 2017.

Previous attendance figures below:
• 2023: [70,747,365](https://www.mlb.com/news/mlb-attendance-mlb-tv-post-big-numbers-in-2023)
• 2024: [71,348,366](https://www.mlb.com/press-release/press-release-mlb-finishes-2024-season-with-highest-attendance-in-seven-years)
• 2025: [71,409,421](https://www.mlb.com/press-release/press-release-mlb-attendance-reaches-71-4-million-three-straight-years-of-growth-for-first-time-since-2007)
questions.market_info_resolution_criteria: This question will resolve as Yes if the official total attendance figure for all MLB regular-season games in 2026 exceeds 72,000,000. This question will resolve according to official reporting by MLB.

questions.question: Will the People's Republic of China blockade or quarantine Taiwan before September 1, 2026?
questions.resolution_criteria: Resolves to the outcome of the question found at https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43707.
questions.background:
CSIS: [How China Could Quarantine Taiwan: Mapping Out Two Possible Scenarios](https://www.csis.org/analysis/how-china-could-quarantine-taiwan-mapping-out-two-possible-scenarios)

> China has significantly increased pressure on Taiwan in recent years. China’s military [frequently flies aircraft](https://chinapower.csis.org/analysis/2022-adiz-violations-china-dials-up-pressure-on-taiwan/) within the island’s air defense identification zone (ADIZ) and across the Taiwan Strait median line. In August 2022 and April 2023, China escalated in response to U.S.-Taiwan political engagements by staging unprecedented large-scale [military exercises](https://chinapower.csis.org/analyzing-chinas-response-to-taiwan-president-tsai-ing-wen-transit/) around Taiwan. China has also [deployed](https://www.cga.gov.tw/GipOpen/wSite/ct?xItem=160424\&ctNode=8195\&mp=9996) its navy and coast guard in increasing numbers around Taiwan and its outlying islands.

> Cross-Strait tensions further intensified in the wake of William Lai’s inauguration as Taiwan’s president on May 20, 2024. Three days after Lai delivered his [inauguration address](https://english.president.gov.tw/News/6726), China commenced two days of large-scale military exercises surrounding Taiwan, called “[Joint Sword-2024A,](https://english.news.cn/20240523/2e8ebbb3171a4f4e96e0147f35fd1df4/c.html)” and accompanied them with “[comprehensive law enforcement operations](https://ccg.gov.cn/lbt/202405/t20240523_2253.html)” (综合执法演练) involving China’s coast guard. Chinese officials [stated](http://eng.mod.gov.cn/xb/News_213114/TopStories/16310652.html) that the drills were intended to “serve as a strong punishment for the separatist acts of ‘Taiwan independence’ forces and a stern warning against the interference and provocation by external forces.”

Please note that military exercises, simulated blockades, or other actions that have the effect of restricting maritime traffic to Taiwan do not count without an accusation as detailed above.

questions.question: Will Pope Leo XIV visit another country other than Spain before September 2026?
questions.resolution_criteria: Resolves to the outcome of the question found at https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43435.
questions.background:
Papal travel outside Rome has historically been rare. Since [Pope Paul VI](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pope_Paul_VI "Pope Paul VI") (1963–1978), however, more pastoral trips have occurred. With advances in technology, mass media and global connectivity, as well as a shift in the Church's leadership, the [Pope's international travel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Papal_travel) has become more common. 

On May 8, 2025, the Papacy of [Pope Leo XIV](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pope_Leo_XIV) commenced after the passing of Pope Francis. Since then [the Pope has visited](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_pastoral_trips_made_by_Pope_Leo_XIV) Turkey, Lebanon, Monaco, Algeria, Cameroon, Angola and Equatorial Guinea. His Holiness is also set to visit [Spain from June 6 to 12](https://www.vatican.va/content/leo-xiv/en/events/event.dir.html/content/vaticanevents/en/2026/6/6/viaggio-spagna.html)[, 2026](https://www.vatican.va/content/leo-xiv/en/events/event.dir.html/content/vaticanevents/en/2026/6/6/viaggio-spagna.html). Although apostolic journeys are often set well in advance, global conflicts, natural disasters and humanitarian crises can precipitate such events.
questions.market_info_resolution_criteria:
This question will resolve as **Yes** if Pope Leo XIV officially visits a country other than Spain or Italy after May 18, 2026 and before September 1, 2026, according to the official [Vatican calendar of events for the Pope](https://www.vatican.va/content/leo-xiv/en/events/year.dir.html/2026.html). Merely passing through a country's airspace, landing at an airport or otherwise making a transitory visit will not count towards resolution.

If Pope Leo XIV ceases to be Pope before September 2026 this question will be **annulled**.

questions.question: Will a new cultivated meat product receive regulatory approval for commercial sale before July 2027?
questions.resolution_criteria: Resolves to the outcome of the question found at https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43492.
questions.background:
Cultivated meat, also known as cultured meat, is genuine animal meat produced by cultivating animal cells in a safe and controlled environment.

[The Good Food Institute](https://gfi.org/science/the-science-of-cultivated-meat/) explains that the manufacturing process begins with acquiring and banking stem cells from an animal. These cells are then grown in bioreactors (known colloquially as [cultivators](https://gfi.org/cultivated/)) at high densities and volumes. The cells are fed an oxygen-rich cell culture medium containing basic nutrients such as amino acids, glucose, fats, vitamins, and inorganic salts, and typically supplemented with growth factors and other proteins.

As of [June 2026](https://gfi.org/resource/where-cultivated-meat-can-be-sold/), cultivated meat can be sold in Singapore, the United States, and Australia. A total of seven companies (UPSIDE Foods, GOOD Meat, Vow, Wildtype, Mission Barns, Believer Meats, and Gourmey/PARIMA) have received regulatory clearance in certain countries to sell multiple different cultivated meat products. The most recent instance was in [April 2026](https://parima.bio/blog/parima-becomes-the-first-european-company-to-obtain-culitvated-food-approval-across-two-species), when PARIMA secured regulatory approval for cultivated duck from the Singapore Food Agency (SFA).
questions.market_info_resolution_criteria:
This question will resolve **Yes** if, after June 30, 2026 and before July 1, 2027, at least one new cultivated meat product receives regulatory approval for commerical sale in any country. The cultivated meat product can be either a blended or standalone product, but must be approved by a national regulatory agency for human consumption and not be the same meat as one with an existing regulatory approval. 

The [Good Food Institute's cultivated meat tracker](https://gfi.org/resource/where-cultivated-meat-can-be-sold/) will be used as the primary resolution source but another credible source may be used in case the tracker becomes unreliable or ceases to exist.

questions.question: Will any U.S. state or EU member state restrict large-scale data center grid connections before September 2026?
questions.resolution_criteria: Resolves to the outcome of the question found at https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43494.
questions.background:
The rapid expansion of AI infrastructure has placed unprecedented pressure on electricity grids across the United States and Europe. The U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA) [projected in January 2026](https://www.eia.gov/outlooks/steo/archives/Jan26.pdf) that electricity demand growth will accelerate significantly, with large computing facilities identified as a primary driver. The U.S. Department of Energy has [similarly identified data centers](https://www.energy.gov/oe/clean-energy-resources-meet-data-center-electricity-demand) as a critical short-term demand factor.

In Europe, grid connection queues [have reached record lengths](https://www.cnbc.com/2026/05/04/denmark-data-centers-moratorium-grid-pause-power-demand.html) in Ireland, the Netherlands, and Denmark — jurisdictions that have previously imposed informal or formal restrictions on new data center connections. Ireland's EirGrid and the Dutch transmission operator TenneT have both taken administrative action to limit new connections in recent years, establishing a precedent base rate for this type of regulatory action.

As of May 2026, several U.S. states including Virginia, Texas, and Georgia — home to the [largest data center concentrations](https://www.pewresearch.org/short-reads/2026/04/13/most-new-data-centers-in-the-us-are-coming-to-rural-areas/) in the country — are actively reviewing grid capacity allocation for high-load facilities.
questions.market_info_resolution_criteria:
This question will resolve as **Yes** if, at any point before September 1, 2026, either a U.S. state or an EU member state announces a new moratorium, suspension, or binding restrictive rule applying to new grid connection requests from large-scale data centers, and the restriction is not officially reversed or rescinded before September 1, 2026, according to [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions).

Large-scale datacenters are defined as facilities with contracted or requested grid capacity of 50 MW or more.

questions.question: Will Shakira's "Dai Dai" outpeak Shakira's "Waka Waka" on the Billboard Hot 100?
questions.resolution_criteria: Resolves to the outcome of the question found at https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43615.
questions.background:
The [2026 FIFA World Cup](https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026) will be co-hosted by the United States, Canada, and Mexico, with matches scheduled from June 11 to July 19, 2026. It will be the first World Cup to feature 48 teams and the first co-hosted by three nations.

On May 14, 2026, FIFA released ["Dai Dai"](https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026/articles/shakira-burna-boy-dai-dai-official-song) by Shakira and Burna Boy as the Official FIFA World Cup 2026 Song, in support of the FIFA Global Citizen Education Fund. The song was unveiled at the Maracana Stadium and released globally across streaming platforms.

The benchmark for this question is Shakira's ["Waka Waka (This Time for Africa)"](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Waka_Waka_\(This_Time_for_Africa\)), the official song of the 2010 FIFA World Cup in South Africa. Waka Waka peaked at **#38 on the US** [Billboard Hot 100](https://www.billboard.com/charts/hot-100/). It became the best-selling World Cup song of all time and one of the most-viewed music videos on YouTube, with multi-platinum certifications in numerous markets.

For comparison, here is how the official songs of the three most recent tournaments performed on the US Hot 100:

* **2014 Brazil** — ["We Are One (Ole Ola)"](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/We_Are_One_\(Ole_Ola\)) by Pitbull, Jennifer Lopez & Claudia Leitte peaked at **#11** on the Hot 100 (the only post-2010 World Cup song to outpeak Waka Waka).
* **2018 Russia** — ["Live It Up"](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Live_It_Up_\(Nicky_Jam_song\)) by Nicky Jam, Will Smith & Era Istrefi did not chart on the Hot 100.
* **2022 Qatar** — ["Hayya Hayya (Better Together)"](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hayya_Hayya_\(Better_Together\)) and ["Tukoh Taka"](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tukoh_Taka) neither charted on the Hot 100.
  Several factors may influence "Dai Dai"'s chart performance.

The tournament being co-hosted by the United States, the world's largest music market and the basis for the Billboard Hot 100, could increase the likelihood of a US chart hit. Shakira herself has an established US chart presence, and Burna Boy is one of the most globally successful Afrobeats artists, having previously charted on the Hot 100.
questions.market_info_resolution_criteria:
This question resolves **Yes** if "Dai Dai" by Shakira & Burna Boy achieves a peak position on the Billboard Hot 100 (US) of #37 or higher at any point on or before July 31, 2026.

The peak position will be determined according to the weekly [Billboard Hot 100 charts](https://www.billboard.com/charts/hot-100/).

questions.question: Will the Save Our Bacon Act be enacted into law before July 2027?
questions.resolution_criteria: Resolves to the outcome of the question found at https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43616.
questions.background:
The [Save Our Bacon Act (H.R. 4673)](https://www.congress.gov/bill/119th-congress/house-bill/4673) was introduced by Rep. Ashley Hinson (R-IA) on July 23, 2025. It would prohibit states and localities from imposing production-process standards (e.g., minimum confinement space) on livestock products produced outside the state, effectively preempting [California's Proposition 12](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2018_California_Proposition_12) and [Massachusetts' Question 3](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2016_Massachusetts_Question_3). It is similar to the [EATS Act](https://www.congress.gov/bill/119th-congress/house-bill/4797) and traces back to the "[King Amendment](https://awionline.org/awi-quarterly/2013-fall/king-amendment%E2%80%99s-attack-state-animal-welfare-laws)" of earlier Congresses.

On April 30, 2026, the House passed [the Farm, Food, and National Security Act of 2026](https://www.congress.gov/bill/119th-congress/house-bill/7567) (the 2026 Farm Bill) by 224–200, with Save Our Bacon language included. The Rules Committee blocked a bipartisan Luna–Costa amendment that would have stripped the language. The bill is now before the Senate, where Agriculture Committee Chairman John Boozman (R-AR) is shepherding consideration.
questions.market_info_resolution_criteria:
This question will resolve as **Yes** if, before July 1, 2027, federal legislation is enacted into law that preempts state regulation of out-of-state pork production.

Specifically, the enacted text must establish that pork producers may raise and market their products in interstate commerce, free from state or local production-process standards imposed on pigs physically raised outside the state's borders. The statute may also cover other livestock (e.g., cattle, veal, dairy), but pork coverage is what this question turns on.

questions.question: Will shipping traffic through the Strait of Hormuz return to normal on any day before September 2026?
questions.resolution_criteria: Resolves to the outcome of the question found at https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43681.
questions.background:
The [Strait of Hormuz](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Strait_of_Hormuz) is a narrow waterway that connects the Persian Gulf to the ocean, via the Gulf of Oman. The strait is bordered by Iran to the north and Oman and the United Arab Emirates to the south. Due to the amount of oil production in the Persian Gulf, 25% of the world's seaborne oil trade, as well as 20% of the world's liquefied natural gas, passes through the strait, with an average of [60 ships](https://hormuzstraitmonitor.com/) historically transiting the strait each day.

On February 28, 2026, Israel and the United States began [a sustained campaign of airstrikes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2026_Iran_war) against Iran, killing Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei. The strikes began a regional war, with Iran retaliating against Israel and the United States, as well as their assets in countries throughout the region. Shortly after the war began, Iran declared that ships would not be allowed to transit the strait, and on March 1, 2026, Iran [attacked several oil tankers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2026_Strait_of_Hormuz_crisis).

Since then, traffic through the strait has been sporadic and [far below normal](https://portwatch.imf.org/pages/cb5856222a5b4105adc6ee7e880a1730). On March 3, 2026, U.S. President Donald Trump announced that the U.S. government would insure and protect traffic through the strait, though the announced insurance program has [failed to get off the ground](https://www.ft.com/content/eabadd1a-a712-4b44-99bf-bb50eb753811). Soon thereafter, U.S. officials announced that [Iran was laying mines in the strait](https://www.nytimes.com/2026/03/12/world/middleeast/iran-mines-strait-of-hormuz-us.html), conduct which has [apparently continued](https://www.axios.com/2026/04/23/iran-strait-hormuz-mines-trump), and which the U.S. has attempted to thwart.

On April 7, 2026, after weeks of secret negotiations, President Trump announced that the U.S. would [suspend its bombing campaign](https://truthsocial.com/@realDonaldTrump/posts/116365796713313030) against Iran in exchange for the opening of the strait. And on April 18, there was a brief but [noticeable uptick](https://portwatch.imf.org/pages/cb5856222a5b4105adc6ee7e880a1730) in the number of ships transiting the strait. After further talks failed to end the war, however, Trump announced that the U.S. would [impose its own blockade](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2026_United_States_naval_blockade_of_Iran) of the strait, and traffic once again decreased.

On May 23, 2026, Trump announced that the two sides were again close to a deal, but shortly thereafter the United States [conducted additional strikes](https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/c4g44yl7q70o) against Iranian vessels that were said to be laying mines in the strait, [decreasing hope for a short-term resolution](https://www.npr.org/2026/05/26/nx-s1-5834840/iran-lebanon-updates) to the conflict.

In the meantime, the long-term closure of the strait has led to a [global fuel crisis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2026_Iran_war_fuel_crisis).
questions.market_info_resolution_criteria: This question will resolve as **Yes** if, after June 15, 2026 and before September 1, 2026, 45 or more ships transit through the Strait of Hormuz during a single day, as reported by [IMF Portwatch](https://portwatch.imf.org/pages/cb5856222a5b4105adc6ee7e880a1730).

questions.question: Will July 2026 be the warmest July on record, according to Copernicus?
questions.resolution_criteria: Resolves to the outcome of the question found at https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43992.
questions.background:
According to the Copernicus Climate Change Service (C3S):

> The C3S mission is to support adaptation and mitigation policies of the European Union by providing consistent and authoritative information about climate change. We offer free and open access to climate data and tools based on the best available science. We listen to our users and endeavour to help them meet their goals in dealing with the impacts of climate change.

July 2023 set a record for the warmest July at the time, with a global average surface air temperature of 16.95°C, which was 0.72°C above the 1991–2020 average and approximately 1.48°C above the 1850–1900 pre-industrial baseline.

July 2024 was the second-warmest July on record at 16.91°C, which was 0.68°C above 1991–2020 (only 0.04°C cooler than July 2023).

July 2025 was 16.68°C, the third warmest July at the time. 

In 2026, monthly anomalies vs. 1991–2020 have been moderate compared to 2023–24:

\- February 2026: +0.53°C (5th warmest February)

\- March 2026: +0.53°C (4th warmest March)

\- April 2026: +0.52°C (3rd-warmest April)

In the second half of 2026, the El Niño Southern Oscillation (ENSO) is [forecast](https://www.noaa.gov/news-release/el-nino-forms-expected-to-strengthen-say-noaa-forecasters) to transition to a "very strong" El Niño, which could put significant upward pressure on global temperatures.
questions.market_info_resolution_criteria: This question resolves as **Yes** July 2026 is the warmest July on record in the ERA5 [dataset](https://cds.climate.copernicus.eu/datasets/reanalysis-era5-single-levels?tab=overview), according to the Copernicus Climate Change Service (C3S) monthly [Climate Bulletin](https://climate.copernicus.eu/climate-bulletin) covering July 2026.

questions.question: Will Starlink's website show South Africa as a country with Starlink availability before September 1, 2026?
questions.resolution_criteria: Resolves to the outcome of the question found at https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43708.
questions.background:
SpaceX's Starlink, now available in over 160 countries, has struggled for regulatory approval in South Africa, a controversy which has drawn the [ire](https://x.com/elonmusk/status/2043280270238101890?lang=en) of SpaceX's founder and controlling shareholder, Elon Musk. According to [Advanced Television](https://www.advanced-television.com/2026/04/21/starlink-struggling-for-approval-on-south-africa-india/): 

> SpaceX is held back by South Africa’s BEE (Black Economic Empowerment) regulations, which – in essence – require any foreign business to have a 30 ‘black’ ownership. Musk has frequently offered about cash to deliver free high-speed internet to 5,000 rural schools in South Africa. This move was in response to a comment by South Africa’s president Cyril Ramaphosa having said that if Musk doesn’t want to comply with BEE requirements, there are alternative pathways—such as investing in education.

> Starlink is reported to have offered up to 2 million Rand (€96m) to expand broadband access in the Southern African Development Community region, according to The Africa Report.

> A research study by the World Bank showed that every 10 per cent increase in broadband penetration can raise GDP growth by 1.21 per cent in countries such as South Africa.

> While urban residents in cities and towns can access broadband by fibre and wireless, rural communities are being held back. South African farmers and rural communities are asking the government to approve Starlink. In many remote areas, there is no internet at all.

At the time of this question, South Africa is not available anywhere in its territory, and "Service date is unknown at this time."

questions.question: Will Iceland vote Yes in the 2026 EU negotiations referendum?
questions.resolution_criteria: Resolves to the outcome of the question found at https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43822.
questions.background:
According to tradition, in 874, [Ingólfr Arnarson](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ing%C3%B3lfr_Arnarson) and his family became the first Icelanders when they set off from what is now Norway and settled in what would become Reykjavík. Over the next thousand years, Iceland would be independent, part of Norway, part of the [Kalmar Union](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kalmar_Union), and part of Denmark, before becoming an independent republic in 1944 following [a referendum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1944_Icelandic_constitutional_referendum) in which over 99% of over 70,000 voters opted to dissolve ties with Denmark and adopt a new constitution.

However Iceland has in many ways retained political ties to the continent. In 1950, shortly after its formation, Iceland joined the Council of Europe. And although never part of the European Union (EU) or its predecessor, the European Economic Community, Iceland in 1970 joined the [European Free Trade Association](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/European_Free_Trade_Association), in 1994 joined the [European Economic Area](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/European_Economic_Area), and in 1996 joined the [Schengen Area](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Schengen_Area). Thus, for decades, Icelanders have participated in a common market and travel area with virtually all EU member states.

In 2008, after Iceland fell into a [severe financial crisis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2008%E2%80%932011_Icelandic_financial_crisis), the prime minister resigned, and snap elections were called. In the run-up to [the election](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2009_Icelandic_parliamentary_election), the interim prime minister, Johanna Sigurðardóttir, stated that, if reelected, she would prioritize EU membership as a way of helping Iceland emerge from the crisis. In the end, her Social Democratic Alliance received the most votes, and she continued on as prime minister, and within months Iceland had applied to join the EU.

EU accession negotiations began in 2010, but public opinion began to sour on the idea, and, in 2013, after closing 11 out of 33 acquis chapters, Iceland [elected](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2013_Icelandic_parliamentary_election) a Euroskeptic government, which suspended Iceland's accession process and dissolved the negotiating committee. Iceland's application to join the EU, though never formally withdrawn, has been frozen ever since.

After the [2024 elections](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2024_Icelandic_parliamentary_election), the Social Democratic Alliance regained the premiership, and the new government declared that Iceland would have another referendum, about whether to resume the EU application process. [That referendum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2026_Icelandic_European_Union_membership_negotiations_referendum) is scheduled to take place on August 29, 2026.
questions.market_info_resolution_criteria: This question will resolve as **Yes** if *Já.* (Yes.) receives the most votes in the 2026 [referendum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2026_Icelandic_European_Union_membership_negotiations_referendum) on whether Iceland should resume accession negotiations with the European Union.

questions.question: Will the Brookmont Catastrophic Bond ETF drop at least $0.25 within two days before September 2026?
questions.resolution_criteria: Resolves to the outcome of the question found at https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43824.
questions.background:
Catastrophe bonds are insurance-linked securities that let insurers and reinsurers transfer the risk of natural disasters to capital-market investors. In calm periods investors collect an attractive yield; on the other hand, they can lose principal if a covered event (generally a hurricane, flood, earthquake, or wildfire) breaches the bond's predefined trigger.

The [Brookmont Catastrophic Bond ETF](https://ilsetf.com/ils) (NYSEARCA: ILS), launched in April 2025 as the first US-listed catastrophe bond ETF, holds a diversified portfolio of [roughly 102 such bonds](https://seekingalpha.com/article/4910536-ils-year-of-existence-revisiting-this-catastrophe-bond-fund-downgrade), heavily weighted toward US exposure. ILS is actively managed and its holdings may change at the discretion of its managers. ILS has little sensitivity to interest-rate fluctuations as its yield is floating-rate.

ILS trades near \$20 and has been remarkably stable, with its entire 52-week range spanning under a dollar. A drop of \$0.25 (about 1.25%) would therefore be an outsized move for this fund, most plausibly driven by a major catastrophe forcing markdowns across exposed holdings or by a broad repricing of catastrophe bond risk. A decline of that size would imply a disaster severe enough to threaten billions in insured losses, on the scale that has historically dented the entire catastrophe bond market.

The window of this question coincides with peak danger months: the Atlantic hurricane season began June 1 and runs into November, while Western US wildfire activity typically intensifies through summer and early fall.
questions.market_info_resolution_criteria: This question will resolve as **Yes** if, on any trading day after June 15, 2026 and before September 1, 2026, the Adjusted Close price of the Brookmont Catastrophic Bond ETF (NYSEARCA: ILS), as reported by [Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/ILS/), is at least \$0.25 lower than its Adjusted Close on the most recent trading day at least two calendar days earlier.

questions.question: Will a Middle Eastern government publicly announce a new agreement permanently stationing Chinese PLA-N naval vessels or personnel on its territory by 31 December 2026?
questions.resolution_criteria: Resolves to the outcome of the question found at https://www.randforecastinginitiative.org/questions/1775.
questions.background:
China operates two confirmed overseas military bases: one established in Djibouti in 2017, and a second at Cambodia's Ream naval base, which the Pentagon's 2025 annual report on Chinese military power formally described as China's second publicly acknowledged overseas military outpost (Newsweek). Chinese navy (i.e., PLA-N) corvettes have been stationed on a rotating basis at Ream since at least December 2023, and in April 2025 China and Cambodia jointly inaugurated a new logistics and training center there, completing a China-funded expansion that included a deep-water pier and dry dock (Stars and Stripes, The Diplomat). Analysts note that visiting third-country vessels have been directed to a separate section of the base from the pier used by Chinese ships, and the extent of any permanent Chinese presence on the northern portion of the base remains unclear (CSIS Asia Maritime Transparency Initiative). The Pentagon's 2025 report assessed that China is seeking to establish additional overseas sites as part of a global logistics and basing network, and listed the United Arab Emirates among countries where China "has likely considered" establishing a basing presence (Newsweek). 

 

Resolution Criteria: 

This question will be resolved using official government statements (from either the host nation or the People's Republic of China) or credible, publicly available reporting on a signed agreement. Relevant official sources include the Chinese Ministry of National Defense and the official government communications portals of Middle Eastern states. Credible secondary reporting from established outlets include (but are not limited to) Reuters, Associated Press, and major regional news organizations.

This question resolves “Yes” if, on or before 31 December 2026, a Middle Eastern government makes a formal public statement, or a verifiable agreement is published, explicitly permitting the permanent stationing of People’s Liberation Army Navy (PLA-N) naval vessels and/or Chinese military naval personnel on its territory. A list of qualifying “Middle Eastern countries” is below:

  • Bahrain
  • Egypt
  • Iran 
  • Iraq
  • Israel
  • Jordan
  • Libya
  • Kuwait
  • Lebanon
  • Oman
  • Qatar
  • Saudi Arabia
  • Syria
  • UAE
  • Yemen

For purposes of this question, “permanent stationing” means an agreement explicitly providing for ongoing or indefinite presence, as distinct from time-limited exercises, temporary port calls, or single-rotation deployments.

The following will NOT be sufficient for resolution:

  • Unilateral U.S. government assessments or intelligence reports alleging Chinese military presence without a corresponding public acknowledgment by the host government or China
  • Port visits, temporary ship calls, or joint naval exercises that do not constitute a formal permanent stationing agreement
  • Reporting based solely on anonymous sources without corroborating official documentation or statements
  • Agreements or arrangements limited to Djibouti or Pakistan, which are explicitly excluded from this question's scope

questions.question: Will a U.S. or U.S.-ally satellite be permanently disabled by another country or organization before 1 January 2027?
questions.resolution_criteria: Resolves to the outcome of the question found at https://www.randforecastinginitiative.org/questions/1644.
questions.background:
Space has increasingly become a contested domain as major powers develop sophisticated anti-satellite (ASAT) capabilities targeting both military and civilian satellites. Russia, China, India, and the United States have all demonstrated kinetic ASAT capabilities by destroying their own satellites in tests, while Russia has allegedly begun developing a nuclear space-based anti-satellite weapon that could disable hundreds of satellites in a wide area using through radiation effects or electromagnetic pulse (Wikipedia, CSIS). U.S. Space Force officials have stated that American satellites face daily “reversible” attacks (i.e., attacks that don’t cause permanent damage) from adversaries, including electronic jamming, laser blinding, cyber attacks, and close approaches by other satellites (TWZ). While ASAT systems have not yet been utilized to permanently disable an adversary's satellite, the proliferation of these capabilities and increasing geopolitical tensions have made their potential use more likely.

Resolution Criteria:
This question will be resolved using official statements from the U.S. government (e.g., U.S. Space Force or U.S. Space Command), NATO, or the relevant government agencies of U.S. allies, and/or reporting from open source news agencies. This question will resolve “Yes” if a satellite owned or operated by U.S. or U.S.-ally governments or organizations is confirmed to have been permanently disabled by another country, non-state organization, or entity acting on their behalf.

For the purposes of this question:

  • “Permanently disabled” means that the satellite is rendered inoperable and cannot carry out any of its intended mission. Both kinetic (e.g., collision or physical destruction) and non-kinetic (e.g., cyberattacks) will count. If on 1 January 2027, it is unclear whether an attack has left a satellite permanently disabled, the attack will not be sufficient for resolution. 
  • “U.S. allies” refers to NATO members, countries designated as Major Non-NATO Allies by the United States, and Taiwan.

The following will not be sufficient for resolution:  

  • Satellites disabled during military exercises or testing activities by the satellite’s owner 
  • Technical malfunctions, failing components, or end-of-life decommissioning
  • Temporary disruptions that are later restored
  • Damage that allows the satellite to continue functioning in some capacity
  • Collisions with space debris or other satellites not intentionally directed at the satellite

This question was inspired by a suggestion submitted by RFI user Willard in the RFI Question Lab.
questions.market_info_resolution_criteria: The resolution criteria has been updated to clarify that Taiwan is included in the scope of "U.S. allies" for this question. While Taiwan is not formally designated as a major non-NATO ally by the U.S. State Department, Public Law 107-228 states that Taiwan "shall be treated as" a major non-NATO ally under U.S. law. Consequently, Taiwan-owned or operated satellites will be considered "U.S.-ally satellites" for the purposes of this question.
